title: 2025/12/10 - GA [Part 2]
displayTitle: 2025-12-10 - Governance and Administration Committee - Regulatory Systems (Internal Affairs) Amendment Bill [Part 2]
description: Wednesday 10 December 2025 | Governance and Administration Committee Regulatory Systems (Internal Affairs) Amendment Bill Runze Liu Aaron Fox New Zealand Mixed Martial Arts Federation - Adeleina Loto Meleisea Cornerman Sports Advisory - Adeleina Loto Meleisea Peri McCardle New Zealand Council for Civil Liberties - Steven Ensslen, Secretary
descriptionSummary: Regulatory Systems (Internal Affairs) Amendment Bill. Runze Liu. Aaron Fox

chunks.text: I'll just get started with our- (laughs) Okay. (laughs) You're a l- (laughs) long way away. The rails are sleeping you. We'll get started with our public session, as it's just gone, um, past 1:00 PM. We've got our first submitter in person, Bruins Luu. I might have mispronounced that, but please correct me. Welcome. Thank you. We've got, um, received your written submission.

You've got five minutes for your oral submission, so please feel free to use as much of that time as you wish, or leave some time for questions from committee. Over to you. Thank you. Uh, kia ora koutou katoa. For Bruins Luu taku ingoa. It's a great privilege to be able to present, uh, the submission from myself and Scott Favell here today.

Uh, we think that the current regulatory systems, Internal Affairs amendment bill, as it also can pertains to identity services, is an excellent opportunity to introduce a non-compulsory photo ID for, for people who are unable to get a driver's license, due to one reason or another.

And I'm gonna talk you guys through what it means for having an s- government issued ID for someone like myself, who is blind. So I have this story from a f- wee while back, where I went to the supermarket to purchase alcohol. And because it's age restricted, I, the duty manager had to ask me to, um, introduce, uh, to produce my identity, uh, my ID. So I gave them my 18 plus card, and then the duty manager was like, "Oh, I don't know what this is. Do you have a driver's license?" And I was like, "What?" while holding my cane as well.

(laughs) And they were like, "Yeah, do you have your driver's license?" And the moment they said, said that, they brought me back into a, uh, into a very vivid scene from when I was 16, where I tried to get a driver's license. And so, at the time, I went to the optometrist's office to get a medical certificate for it.

And so the optometrist, they, I paid my f- consultation fee, they took me out back, did some tests, and then they were like, "Okay, here's a form. We'll send you on your way." So I took the form, went to the AA office, and then I showed them the medical certificate, and they were like, "Oh, we can't accept this.

It's not signed." And by that point, I was pretty much in tears. So when the duty manager asked me to produce a driver's license, they brought me back into the optometrist's office, where the optometrist took my money, but then didn't tell me the truth and just sent me on my way. They brought me back into the AA office, where I was in tears knowing that, basically, life was gonna be hard, especially without an official gov card style government ID, and I had no say or choice in the matter. So having an official card style government issued photo ID, one with a flag on it,

one with a New Zealand government hologram on it, that can fit in my wallet-... and I can produce anywhere, and I can use it, say, at the bank, or enrolling to vote online without needing a birth certificate or citizenship certificate would make life slightly easier for the up to 400,000 people in New Zealand who are unable to drive due to one reason or another.

And we'll be able to give them a choice in the matter. And I'm happy to take questions. Just check with the committee members, any questions? Andy Foster. Thank you very much. And, um, Ron Soosay, um, spoke to the Transport and Infrastructure Committee a little while ago, I think it might be. Yeah, it was excellent. Um, you did that very, very well, very powerfully, so thank you.

Yes, thank Um, just one quick question you've got there. Um, so you've got, in Western Australia, for example, you've got, um, the same photo cannot be used on both a driver's license and a photo card. Can you just talk us through that? 'Cause the, the one concern people might have is, there's a lot of people who, who are very nervous about any form of identity card. And I know you said voluntary, good.

But, um, what's the, what's the thinking behind having different, um, photo on the two different, two different documents? Oh, it's just for security purposes since, just in case there's scenarios where someone might use the same photo for different, uh, it's, it's similar to why the, like, generally in driver's license and a passport there's different photos, just for security reasons, just as an extra layer of security to protect against possible fraud, identity fraud or theft.

Okay. No, thank you. Thank you so much, uh, Ronzie. Thank you, and also to, um, your, uh, colleague Scott as well for, for that submission. We, we've noted your comments and, um, really appreciate you coming in today, and, and y- for your written submission as well. And it's also going to Trans- Transport and Infrastructure. Thank you so much. Thank you so much. Cool, thank you very much.

We'll now move on to our next submitter who's joining us by Zoom, Aaron Fox. Welcome, Aaron. Thank you. Thank you. We've got, um, five minutes to hear from you. And we have received your written submission, so over to you. Kia ora. Thank you very much. Uh, thank you for the opportunity to talk this afternoon.

Um, pity I couldn't be there in person, but with the rescheduling, uh, here I am on, here I am on Zoom this afternoon. So, with respect to my submission, uh, simply put, I do ask that this committee please recommend that Clause 162 of the Regulatory Systems Bill 2025 be deleted in full.

When I reflect upon the 50 years in which I've worked with military history, I cannot conceive of any circumstance of why, how, or when an exemption from the Public Records Act could ever be warranted. Our national identity is built in part upon our military history. That history is in turn built upon our military archives. Where these archives have gaps, as they do, our collective national memory is all the poorer. Imagine attempting to relate the epic of New Zealand at Gallipoli solely from British and Australian records.

From a multinational perspective, our role at Gallipoli was that of a small cog in a much bigger machine. Only we can tell our distinctive story of Anzac based on that national taonga, the official military and the vet-, and the veterans' archives. Now, Defence Force record keeping almost a generation ago was found to be deficient, if not inept, by the Operation Burnham inquiry. However, rather than committing to rectifying this operational shortcoming, the NZDF seems to seek instead, by means of Clause 162, to reinstate

a current, future and retrospective fix-up to relieve them of responsibility, and excuse the administrative failures detailed in the Operation Burnham report, denying fault rather than correcting it. Now, I contend that risk-averse government agencies are a liability, and our current Commissioner of, of Police is working overtime to eliminate group-think in his own organization.

I think equally the NZDF must enhance its record keeping so that it remains fully accountable for overseas operations within a multinational environment. The regulatory impact statement does not provide a convincing rationale for this clause. Clause 162 itself does not set out any mechanisms to determine cause and effect, checks and balances, or approval and appeal. I consider that the clause has been drafted in haste, and represents an all-considered or dismissive official response to the concerns raised by the Operation Burnham inquiry.

Our government agencies must be accountable, especially when they are operating overseas in a multinational arrangement. Where accountability is in question, especially with respect to full and frank record keeping, then the overseas operation itself should be called into, into question. Excuse me. Handing responsibility for the maintenance of the official New Zealand record over to a third party nation or organization is tantamount to relinquishing our hard-earned sovereignty, our independence of action, and our autonomy of

decision-making, handing that to somebody or something else. The Operation Burnham inquiry made it abundantly clear just how vital efficient and detailed record keeping by New Zealand public officers is, especially when something goes badly wrong. E- equally, a complete and detailed public record is essential when something goes right. Without records, how can our military veterans of the future be properly appreciated and recognized for their service? Without New Zealand's military archives of the future, there may well be no future official histories of New Zealand's role in

peacemaking and peacekeeping. So in conclusion, posterity must first remember us before it can judge us, lest we forget. Thank you. Mm. Thank you so much for your submission. Uh, we've had a couple of other, uh, submissions focused on, um, Clause 162 as well, uh, which I hope you, I don't know if you had the opportunity to hear those earlier today.

I didn't, unfortunately. But I've read the submissions, so yes, I'm in agreement with what's- Yeah. Did you have, did have any, uh-... are in agreement with them. Okay, fantastic. I'll just check if there's any other questions from members. Tim Bosley? I won't ... Sir, um, can I ask you a quick question while you're here, uh, since we just have a couple of minutes of time? Um, from your perspective as a military historian, you spoke about the toga that sort of comes from those generations, do you have any views on, uh, you may

have seen the, the current exhibition that encourages people to walk over the New Zealand flag? Just with that perspective, I just thought, if you had a minute to, to offer that perspective? Fortunately, that's not an official record. Look, I hold with the, with the flag as an emblem of New Zealand. Um, and really, if somebody wants to be controversial about that, fortunately it's not in a, it's not in an official public setting.

It's, it's in an art gallery, I believe. Um, it's not something I would condone. It's not something I would, um, do myself, walking on a flag or burning it or, or displaying it inappropriately. No, it's, um, if it's our national flag, then let us treat it as such. All right. Thanks for that- Uh, New Zealand stood under that equi- equitably. Yeah.

I appreciate it. Thank you. Thank you so much, Aaron. Thank you very much for your submission and for joining us today, and apologies for the, um, changing of the times. That's all right. Thank you for your time. Appreciate it. Thank you so much. Thanks, Aaron. Appreciate that. Thank you. We now have the New Zealand Mixed Martial Arts Federation on Zoom. Is that correct?
Let's check if they got 10 minutes? Um, so there's 300 agencies, I believe one person represented from each part of the mixed, mixed martial arts has another presenter. Oh, okay.

Yes? Welcome, Matt. Thank you. Kia Ora, everyone. Can you hear me okay? Kia Ora. Yes, we can. Mm-hmm. Just checking, are you expecting another, uh, presenter with you? Or just, just you today? Uh, I'll just be presenting, I think. Um, we've got Adelina, uh, listening in as well too. Okay, no worries. Over, over to you.

We've got, uh, two minutes for your submission. Thank you so much. Thank you. Um, yeah, basically, my name's Matt Tau. Um, I represent the New Zealand Mixed Martial Arts Federation. Uh, we're the national body for MMA here in New Zealand. Uh, and we're currently in line with the United, uh, World Wrestling Association and also the Association of Boxing Commissions.

So- Okay.... um, they're our international bodies we're aligned with, and, um, we support the repeal of the Boxing and Wrestling Act. Um, but we cannot res- support the repeal without, uh, a replacement. Um, uh, the problem basically is it's an outdated law. Um, the act was designed originally to control gambling and also, um, uh, gangs, uh, originally, and not to protect the athletes. Um, it never contemplated MMA, uh, or the modern codes as well. Um, under this outdated framework, uh, we've had deaths occur, uh, in combat sports, in training and in

competitions. Uh, we've had many examples of fighters being concussed, uh, in one discipline and then going and complete- competing into another discipline, um, just in within a week's time. Um, there's no mandatory medicals, there's no concussion tracking, and there's no accountability for these things. So, um, we're relying on the good faith and the good operating on a lot of, uh, people organizing competitions.

Um, but there's a lot of people, um, that aren't adhering to basic minimum standards that we feel should be there. So, um, we just think that having some sort of legislation that will help us govern our sports properly, um, will be definitely necessary. Um, the hardest thing probably is we've seen people, uh, uh, that we know personally get fol- uh, involved in serious accidents as well, um, through some of these procedures as well too.

And one of them was in a coma for over a year. Uh, he was our first medal winner for the New Zealand Mixed Martial Arts Federation at the World Championships in 2015. Um, so it was very hard to see him in a coma, um, uh, through one bad, uh, weight management, uh, cutting techniques and also, um, having concussions at training, uh, and also competing in pro boxing fights and being stocked in that, and then competing within a month, uh, in a different, uh, type of sport. So, um, these are the things that we're desperately trying to get a

grip of. And definitely being able to share information, um, between the different codes is a must, but then also on top of that, uh, having something that will force people to take mandatory stand-downs and things like that too. Um, I guess, uh, the solution, um, we've proposed a combat sports commission model, uh, to mandate medical standards and injury, uh, registries, um, also enforce cross-code suspensions, uh, and also lic- license officials and standardize rules amongst, um, promoters, uh, coaches and also athletes as well, so that there's a standard for everyone to

adhere to. Um, co-design is essential. Um, some parts of, uh, Australia's mistakes, uh, was regulating without listening to, uh, the people involved directly in the sports. Um, so we definitely don't want to have to learn the same mistakes that they had to go through. Um, some state commissions there imposed rules that didn't fit the sports, uh, driving events underground and also alienating practitioners as well. So, that's again, something we don't want to repeat here.

Um, yeah, I guess, uh, regulation with the sports, uh, builds trust. Uh, regulation without it, it destroys it. Um, and yeah, we're just trying to build that up again.Uh, in a global context, why it matters that it's not regulated properly, um, we haven't had the UFC return to New Zealand since, uh, 2020. Um, in Australia, state governments now are paying the UFC to host events, uh, because trusted regulation gives confidence in safety, integrity, and also logistics.

Uh, without credible oversight, global promotions won't come here. Uh, they have to import their own regulatory, uh, infrastructure, adding costs and risks as well. Um, so it's already hard enough with us being so isolated to get them over here. Um, the same regulatory c- clarity that protects fighters also attracts in, uh, investment as well, uh, and that's how we grow safely and sustainably. Um, we urge the committee to, uh, do not repeal without replacement, um, and also, uh, New Zealand can do this better than Australia

because we can do it together from the start. And that's something that we believe in as well. Um, yeah, is there any, any questions at all? Just check with, uh, yeah, Andy Foster. Look, thank you very much, Matt. Um, two questions if I can. One is the what degree of, uh, discussion has been across the, um, uh, I guess you could say in this case the martial arts, uh, community in terms of, uh, of this legislation, so what, what conversations you've had. And secondly, just having a quick look at the, um, the Act that's proposed to be withdrawn, essentially it just looks like

a, um, a, a, a system where, uh, any organization essentially applies to the minister and says, "Look, this is the way we're gonna run our organization. Are you happy with it? Yes. Good. Tick. You get a license to, to do it. If, if not, no you don't." Is that kind of the way it- Y- okay, so I'll answer the first one- How big a deal- ... so but, um, yeah, basically, um, we- we've been, we've been bringing this particular situation up s- uh, for the last six years, uh, with Sport New Zealand. Um, we've sent, um, delegates and

representatives over to America to learn how, um, the different states are regulating in, in America, and also, um, she's also been involved in, um, investigating how they do it in Australia quite in- extensively as well, and also places around Europe, particularly in Ireland. So, um, the reason we're so in depth with this is MMA, uh, one of the fastest growing sports in the world, uh, is not actually, uh, covered by any legislation in New Zealand.

And so for us to run our NSO properly, um, we don't actually have any teeth. So if someone decides to be a cowboy, so to speak, and do their own things, and not adhere to our, um, uh, what, what we would like to see, uh, people adhering to, uh, we don't have anything that can actually stop 'em from doing it.

Um, we've had plenty of different examples of, uh, promoters having shows without medical staff there, uh, without the right people, um, doing doctor's checks. And it's just a recipe for disaster. It's an accident waiting for happen. Um, we've also had other examples of, uh, boxing events where police have not given licenses to certain fights, but they've still gone ahead anyway. Uh, and nothing's getting done about it.

So, um, and again, I mentioned the earlier incident of the, um, unfortunate person that had jumped from code to code after being, um, stopped in one code and then competing in another and ending up in a coma. Mm. So what you, what it sounds like is that the existing legislation's not really helping you very much, but you would like some legislation- Definitely.

... and that some shared across the sector, you know, whether it's (overlapping speech) Definitely, and, and-... sports bars (overlapping speech) Yeah. Yeah, so we see a combat sports commission being able to regulate, uh, all of the combat sports properly. Okay. No, thank you. Thanks. This definitely requires more thought.

(laughs) Definitely. Thank you. Uh, we have a question from Lomaunga Lydia Saasima. Uh, thanks for your submission. Uh, it's really helpful to hear from you, not just the written submission. A couple of questions. Uh, you have lobbied or have had (overlapping speech) with Sport New Zealand, uh, some years ago, and is that relationship enduring? Secondly, you've made a set of recommendations- If you don't have to-... um, which is helpful. Um, so sort of where do you see, uh, your sports code if New Zealand Sport is not, uh, or sorry, if Sport New Zealand is not,

uh, or can partner or have further discussions with you? So yeah, basically, um, we've also, uh, been approached by, uh, one of the national MPs for Mount Roskill, um, Dr. Carlos Chung. He's, um, wanted to hear our views on things- He's everywhere (laughs) .

Oh, sorry, what was that? Sorry, there are just some enthusiastic supporters of Carlos, um, at the table, but you can just repeat your- His colleagues.... what you just said. His colleagues. Oh, okay. (laughs) Yeah, so Carlos has approached us and he's been fantastic with asking us, um, uh, what we've had with issues with regards to the current frame, uh, and how we see things, um, being improved as well too. So I guess we're trying as many avenues as we can to get this out there. We feel it's that important that we're just waiting for

injuries to happen in the meantime. Okay. There's too many gray areas where people are just doing their own things and they're not adhering to safe practices and safety codes. Um, we've got 15-year-olds fighting, um, th- uh, 42-year-old professionals for world titles in some events. Um, it's just becoming a bit of a joke and we can't do anything about it right now.

So, um, we do believe that this is something that needs to be fixed ASAP, and it's been one of the highest, um, things that we've been trying to achieve for six years now. Um, just to follow on, uh, from that, so obviously you've got people that are interested, you've got athletes who actually probably need a really good, uh, regulatory framework, but in informing that with, uh, an MP, you would need, uh, a bit more information. Is, do you think that you've got enough to work with, um, Carlos in terms of framing things up?...

'cause you've obviously got a following, you've got some international evidence. Um, you just need a really good robust framework to go through the system, parliamentary system. Correct. Correct. We, we do feel like we have enough, um, information and resources now to actually put this together. Uh, we've talked to, uh, enough of the people in the different, uh, combat sports as well, uh, to work out who the key figures are, and they are actually keen to be involved with this as well too.

Um, but I'll be honest with you, the main push really does come from the MMA community because everyone else is really covered at this stage. So, um, but when it comes to fighter safety, then that's one thing we have all got in common there. Thank you. Yeah. We've just, um, run out of time now, Matt. But thank you so much for a really interesting submission of your engagement with the committee. And we'll now move on to the Quarterman Sports Advisory Committee, which I think, Adelina, you're speaking to.

Welcome to the committee. Uh, y- we've got 10 minutes for your submission. You would have, uh, maybe seen the previous submission, and so you're welcome to use that time or to leave some time for questions. Over to you. Uh, kia ora. Thank you. Just checking you can hear me? Yep. We can hear you. Thank you. Okay, awesome. Um, tena koutou. My name is Adelina.

I'm from Quarterman Sports Advisory. So we work at the intersection of sports, law, and commerce. Um, a, a lot of my submissions will follow on from NZMMAF, um, with the slight difference in that I have more of a commercial POV, or point of view, whereas NZMMAF is purely regulatory. Um, so, uh, I won't speak to every single one of the submissions, but I, uh, I will make, um, highlight which I think are the most important today.

So, uh, my message today is pretty simple. It's just that the, the repeal of the Boxing and Wrestling Act, um, is not a small and minor, minor matter. Um, I did read in Hansard that it was referred to as a rats and mice matter, which, you know, to an extent, the entire bill is.

However, um, i- in regards to the Boxing and Wrestling Act, it's a structural change, um, that will shape whether combat sports in New Zealand are safed- safe, investable, and internationally credible. Um, so, you know, as was said in the last submission, repeal isn't the problem. Um, I, I would, uh, repeal of the act is good.

It's, uh, outdated and not fit for purpose. Um, but what we don't want to happen is repeal without at least a plan for replacement. Um, without any sort of framework, we revert to an unregulated gray zone, and what tends to happen there is that there'll be police discretion deciding what goes ahead.

Um, and that sort of uncertainty for commercial actors in particular, like, uh, promoters, it discourages, it discourages promoters from, um, from operating within this industry and that, and that invites risk, um, in the types of characters which are attracted to, um, participate in an unregulated industry. So, we've seen overseas that regulation enables commerce.

So the UFC was mentioned in the previous, um, submission. That's the Ultimate Fighting Championship, but they're the main MMA, um, promotion worldwide, and they are responsible for the growth of MMA, um, worldwide. They pushed for that for about 10 years, and it really didn't start getting traction until the Nevada State Athletic Commission, um, started regulating very seriously, and that has now grown into a multi-billion dollar industry.

Right. Um, as was mentioned as well, the UFC hasn't staged an event here since 2020. There's obviously a f- a lot of factors for that, but one of those, and I know this from conversations had with regulators in the States, um, uh, many promotions would prefer to come to a, to a market where there is trusted regulation, um, and internationally aligned regulation in place. Because first of all, it means they don't have to fly people all the way over from America to regulate their shows, um, and it just makes it a lot easier for them in that

sense. Um, i- one point, if you don't remember anything I say today, uh, I, it would be this, is that, please do not, uh, follow our t- the tendency to just see what our brothers over across the ditch have done and, and implement that. Um, not all Australian models for combat sport regulation have worked effectively.

In some of their states, regulation was imposed without consultation with industry, and in a- as a result, that alienated practitioners, over-complicated compliance, and it, and it fractured the combat community. So, that's what we don't want to happen here in New Zealand. Um, there is a real opportunity for New Zealand.

You know, we have such a, where there's a geographic isolation, that's clear. We're never gonna overcome that. Um, but it's more, uh, we could see it as an opportunity rather than as an issue. So our small size lets us integrate safety, governance, and commerce in one model. And there's nothing to say that we couldn't design a combat sports commission alongside, you know, with government alongside with the combat sport industry, um, in a way that large jurisdictions just aren't able to do easily. So, that's potential for New Zealand to create something

novel, um, and become a trusted global destination for, for combat sports. So-You know, just to— uh, in conclusion, repeal should not mean deregulation. It should mean modernisation. Um, some sort of combat sports regulation, uh, whether it's an act or a commission that pro- protects athletes, gives promoters confidence, and positions New Zealand on the world stage. Safety, integrity, and commerce are not competing objectives. They're interdependent, and pro- you know, present New Zealand with a great opportunity. And New Zealand, as we know, um, we're

storied for creating and developing and exporting incredible sporting talent. There's nothing to say we can't do the same with, um, sporting business, sporting structure, sporting governance. Um, so I'll leave it there, if there are any questions.

Thank you so much. We do have a couple of questions. We'll start with the Maunga Lydia Sosene and then go to Andy Foster. Thanks for your submission. Uh, it's really helpful to understand sort of the overall high-level, um, trajectory that you've talked about. The modernisation, just two questions. Modernisation, because there's no sort of facts and figures, um, it's quite a general, uh, overall that you're asking the committee to strongly consider, not completely, uh, letting go of it.

But your request is to modernise. Second question is around the numbers. Um, are you... I mean, I don't have any understanding of how big it is, uh, with different codes in New Zealand. Is that what you're asking the committee? 'Cause it'd be helpful that you're really clear, um, to say to the committee exactly what you're after.

So, um, it's just a little bit hard to judge 'cause you're talking more from a position or recommendation not to repeal it, but to modernise it. And it would be helpful to have sort of further information, although the committee won't have any time to do that. So, what is the thing that you would be focusing on given your sort of like the business side of it?

Okay. So, as was, you know, referred to in the previous submission, um, uh, the advocacy for reform in this space has been going on for some time without any traction. Um, that, you know, Dr Carlos Cheung being, uh, you know, he's proactively reached out to different actors in the, in the combat sports community. That we would see as progress, um, and that is really the realistic, um, pathway forward, um, is, you know, if select committee is able to make a recommendation that yes, repeal this act, however, have, you know, what

is... I-i-it can't just be that the act is repealed and then it, things just fade into this grey zone. There has to be some sort of push to say these conversations with, you know, headed by Dr Carlos Cheung or whoever needs to, need to be, um, followed up or implore Sport New Zealand, um, to get involved in this conversation more seriously. Um, you know, whether there's some sort of panel, uh, amongst all the different combat sport codes because MMA is one sport, then of course there's the very important boxing. Then there are other sports which don't even have national bodies

which are recognised, so like Muay Thai kickboxing. Um, it, it's just, it... There needs to be some sort of clear plan for contacting every single one of those combat codes, having a conversation with them, getting them to work together about what could this work? What could this look like working together?

S- Uh, because only in doing that will it become clear what some sort of collaborative co- co- co-designed combat sports commission could look like. And any, any of those things that I've just said haven't been done at all in the last however many years. So, whatever power or influence the select committee has to suggest, um, that that happens following the repeal of the act, uh, is what I would say needs to happen.

Thank you. That's very helpful. Thank you. We've got another question from Andy Foster. Yeah, thanks Adelina. Um, two, uh... First thing is, you're not, you're... So you're not particularly invested at all in the existing legislation? No. No. Then I'm saying existing legislation doesn't do the business. Okay, that's nice and clear. So the, the... So are the models... You said don't look across Tasman. Um, but are the models...

I mean, the, the... When, whenever you said the word commission, that suggests an American model, but, um, is, is there a particular model you've looked at and said that's a good start to think about, um, what are the regulatory model... I would suggest that, that perhaps Canada, that, um, you know, it's put forward that you look to how Canada...

Okay. Um, Canada look... Sorry, regulates combat sports. Um, and then there are certainly smaller states within America, um, because we're never gonna be as big as say California or New York. Um, but the difference can be, you know, New York is extremely, um, uh, focused on compliance, whereas smaller jurisdictions probably fit New Zealand better.

Um, and so yeah, it's just a matter of consulting the right people who actually know that stuff, um, and, and getting that conversation going. Also, the head, the commissioner of the California State Commission, his name is, um, Andy Foster. Just wanted to say that. Oh, very good. (laughs) We'd better go and talk to him then.

Yeah. (laughs) Well, thank you- There's tension. Hey, thank you.... so much, um, Adelina for your, uh, very comprehensive, um, and interesting submission. Uh, so thanks very much. We've noted your comments today. Thank you. Thank you. Thanks very much. We've got our next, uh, Perry McArdle, who's (...) Welcome, Gary. Thank you for your written submission, which we've received and we've got five minutes today.

Uh, so if you want to use all of that time, we'll leave some time for questions. Over to you. Hi, uh, thank you for having me. Uh, my name is Perry, and for a bit of background, my husband and I began our fertility journey back in 2018, so about seven years ago. Um, and it was really important to me to make a submission today because it's rare to have this opportunity, um, to flag an issue with the Heart Act, and this is legislation that, in my view, needs updating to reflect the realities that families face today.

Um, I will be focusing on one specific issue, and while this was not originally, uh, in the proposed changes, I would like to see this change considered, uh, and that is Section 10 of the Heart Act. And Section 10 sets out a 10-year storage limit for embryos and gametes in New Zealand.

As the law currently stands, ECART, the ethics committee, can only approve an extension for storage of embryos or gametes before the storage period expires, uh, and on top of that, the storage period includes any time they have been stored overseas. Uh, and once that 10 years has passed, even by a short amount of time, ECART loses all legal authority to consider an application at all. Uh, doesn't matter how reasonable or ethical that, uh, circumstance may be. I did want to note, I don't personally believe that our specific case, which you would have read

about, um, was ever an intended outcome when the act was written. I think our specific case is an unintended failure and unfortunately, when this provision fails someone, there's currently no process to correct it. Um, so what you would have seen in our submission is that our gametes, uh, were stored overseas in Canada as part of a cancer-related treatment. Before they expired, though I will note we were unaware of any expiration, um, law, we sought the advice here in New Zealand.

We approached our GP, we met with a fertility specialist, and neither raised to us that New Zealand had this strict 10-year law. Um, and then by the time we attempted to transfer our gametes home, we learned we were now five years past the limit, uh, at which time ECART could not help us, not because they disagreed with our case, but because the legislation gave them no legal ability to even consider it. Um, and as a result, at 27 and 31, we lost the ability to use our own reproductive tissue in New Zealand. Um, and again, this is solely due to the rigid

application of this timeframe and the absence of any post-expiry mechanism, uh, currently in the act, of which there is none. Um, so this highlights some key issues. The law prevents ECART from doing its job as an ethics body, in my opinion, um, and patients can lose access to their own tissue, specifically New Zealanders, uh, like ourselves.

We're not talking about donor tissue; we're talking about our own personal human tissue, um, and that's not necessarily because of ethical concerns, but because of timing technicalities. Um, and I did want to note that the strict 10-year limit is not necessarily the global standard.

Uh, in Canada, where thankfully our gametes are currently stored, there's no statutory time limit. Consent is what governs use, and in Australia, several states, including Queensland, who most recently updated their policy last year, do not impose a fixed storage limit at all. Um, for some final words, because I did want to leave time if there was a question, um, I wanted to share the exact words that ECART wrote to us when they were forced to decline our application of extension.

They wrote, and I quote, "Unfortunately, ECART is not infrequently placed in a position where it considers it has no choice but to decline an application for an extension of storage on the basis that the Heart Act does not allow it to consider applications which are received more than 10 years after the initial storage date.

ECART has raised its concerns about the restrictive nature of these provisions and the impact of their application with ACART. ECART is an advocate for and would fully support legislative change to allow ECART the discretion to consider extending storage in circumstances such as yours."
